concept: The Event Loop: Call Stack vs Task Queue

executionTimeline.description: The script starts executing. 'Sync 1' is pushed to the call stack and logged immediately.
setTimeout is called. The timer is registered with the OS. The callback is scheduled to be moved to the Timers Queue after 0ms.
process.nextTick is called. The callback is placed into the special NextTick Queue, which has the highest priority in Node.js.
'Sync 2' is pushed to the call stack and logged immediately.
The current synchronous block is finished. Before moving to any event loop phase, Node.js immediately drains the NextTick Queue.
The Event Loop enters the 'Timers' phase. It sees the expired setTimeout callback and pushes it to the stack.

theFix: To avoid 'starving' the event loop, never call process.nextTick() recursively. If you need a periodic task, use setImmediate() or setTimeout(), which allow the event loop to move through its other phases (like I/O and Poll) between executions.
